docs.title: Backend Engineering Notes
docs.text: A personal knowledge base covering backend engineering topics, system design, and production experience.

docs.title: Main Sections
docs.text:
• Interview Preparation — a structured 14-day interview roadmap
  • Java — collections, JVM internals, concurrency, and core language topics
  • Spring — Spring Core and Spring Boot concepts
  • Persistence — Hibernate and JPA notes
  • Databases — transactions, isolation, indexing, and optimization
  • Messaging — Kafka and event-driven systems
  • Kubernetes — deployment and platform concepts
  • System Design — architecture and scalability

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This repository serves as:

  • a long-term engineering reference
  • a structured learning resource
  • a collection of interview preparation notes
  • a place to document practical production knowledge

docs.title: Interview Preparation
docs.text:
This section turns your notes into a more structured interview-preparation roadmap for a senior Java engineer.

The goal is not to relearn everything from zero, but to refresh the core concepts, strengthen the internals, and practice explaining them clearly.

docs.title: Recommended Study Pattern
docs.text:
For each day, follow this rhythm:

  1. 30 minutes — refresh the theory
  2. 45 minutes — go deeper into internals and edge cases
  3. 45 minutes — answer 20–30 interview questions aloud
  4. 30 minutes — solve one coding or design problem

docs.title: Day 01 — JVM & Memory
docs.text:
Theory

  • JVM architecture
  • Stack vs heap
  • Metaspace
  • Stack frames
  • Class loading
  • Java Memory Model

Practice

  • 25 interview questions
  • Review memory-related bugs and GC behavior

docs.title: Day 02 — Collections
docs.text:
Theory

  • List, Set, Map, Queue
  • HashMap, ConcurrentHashMap, TreeMap, LinkedHashMap
  • equals/hashCode
  • Rehashing and load factor
  • Red-black trees and collisions

Practice

  • 30 interview questions
  • Compare collection choices for different scenarios

docs.title: Day 03 — Concurrency I
docs.text:
Theory

  • Thread lifecycle
  • synchronized
  • volatile
  • AtomicInteger
  • LongAdder
  • ReentrantLock
  • ReadWriteLock
  • Semaphore
  • CountDownLatch
  • CyclicBarrier

Practice

  • 30 interview questions
  • Focus on visibility, atomicity, and coordination

docs.title: Day 04 — Concurrency II
docs.text:
Theory

  • ThreadPoolExecutor
  • ExecutorService
  • ForkJoinPool
  • CompletableFuture
  • Virtual Threads
  • Structured concurrency
  • Parallel streams

Practice

  • One multithreading exercise
  • One real-world concurrency scenario

docs.title: Day 05 — Core Java Language
docs.text:
Theory

  • OOP fundamentals
  • SOLID principles
  • Generics
  • Reflection
  • Annotations
  • Exceptions
  • Records
  • Sealed classes
  • Pattern matching
  • Optional

Practice

  • Review common interview questions on language features

docs.title: Day 06 — Functional Java
docs.text:
Theory

  • Streams
  • Collectors
  • Lambdas
  • Functional interfaces
  • Method references

Practice

  • Stream exercises
  • Compare imperative vs functional solutions

docs.title: Day 07 — Mock Interview
docs.text:
Focus

  • 70–100 questions
  • Review weak areas
  • Practice speaking clearly under pressure

docs.title: Day 08 — Spring Core
docs.text:
Theory

  • IoC
  • Dependency Injection
  • Bean lifecycle
  • Bean scopes
  • Profiles
  • AOP

Practice

  • Explain bean creation and lifecycle flow

docs.title: Day 09 — Spring Boot
docs.text:
Theory

  • Auto-configuration
  • Starter dependencies
  • Configuration properties
  • Actuator
  • Validation
  • REST APIs
  • Exception handling

Practice

  • One Spring Boot design or debugging scenario

docs.title: Day 10 — Hibernate & JPA
docs.text:
Theory

  • Entity lifecycle
  • Persistence context
  • Dirty checking
  • Flush and clear
  • Fetch types
  • N+1 problem
  • Caching
  • Transactions

Practice

  • Common Hibernate interview questions

docs.title: Day 11 — Database Essentials
docs.text:
Theory

  • Isolation levels
  • Locking
  • Indexes
  • Query optimization
  • Execution plans
  • Connection pooling

Practice

  • Explain how you would debug a slow query

docs.title: Day 12 — Microservices & Messaging
docs.text:
Theory

  • REST communication
  • Kafka
  • RabbitMQ
  • Redis
  • API gateway
  • Circuit breaker
  • Resilience patterns
  • CAP theorem
  • Event-driven architecture

Practice

  • One architecture discussion
  • One messaging tradeoff question

docs.title: Day 13 — System Design
docs.text:
Theory

  • Scalability
  • Caching
  • Load balancers
  • Database scaling
  • Sharding
  • Consistency
  • High availability

Practice

  • Design a simple scalable system end to end

docs.title: Day 14 — Final Mock Interview
docs.text:
Focus

  • Java
  • Spring
  • Hibernate
  • Architecture
  • Coding
  • Behavioral questions

docs.title: Final Advice
docs.text:
Since you already have strong practical experience, prioritize:

  • internals over basics
  • clear explanations over memorization
  • real-world examples over textbook answers
  • structured practice over passive reading

docs.title: Question
docs.text: What is the difference between HashMap, ConcurrentHashMap, and Hashtable?

docs.title: Answer
docs.text:
HashMap is a non-thread-safe implementation of the Map interface that stores data as key-value pairs. Internally it uses hashing to distribute entries across buckets, which allows O(1) average lookup performance.

ConcurrentHashMap is a thread-safe implementation designed for highly concurrent environments. Unlike Hashtable, it does not lock the entire map for every operation. Modern implementations use a combination of CAS operations and fine-grained synchronization, allowing multiple threads to read and update the map concurrently with much better scalability.

Hashtable is a legacy thread-safe implementation. Most operations synchronize on the entire map, meaning only one thread can effectively modify or access protected operations at a time. Because of this, it typically performs worse than ConcurrentHashMap under contention.

In modern applications, ConcurrentHashMap is generally preferred over Hashtable when thread safety is required.

docs.title: HashMap
docs.text:
When inserting a value:

map.put("John", 100);

HashMap:

  1. Calculates the key's hashcode.
  2. Maps the hashcode to a bucket.
  3. Stores the entry in that bucket.

When retrieving:

map.get("John");

the same hash calculation is performed, allowing the map to jump directly to the correct bucket.

This is why average lookup complexity is O(1).

docs.title: Hash Collisions
docs.text:
Multiple keys may end up in the same bucket.

Before Java 8, collisions were stored as linked lists.

Since Java 8, large collision chains can be converted into balanced trees, improving worst-case lookup performance.

docs.title: ConcurrentHashMap
docs.text:
ConcurrentHashMap improves concurrency by:

  • Using CAS operations for many updates.
  • Applying synchronization only where necessary.
  • Allowing mostly lock-free reads.

This significantly reduces contention compared to Hashtable.

docs.title: Hashtable
docs.text:
Hashtable synchronizes most operations using a single monitor.

Effectively:

public synchronized V get(...)

public synchronized V put(...)

Every operation competes for the same lock.

docs.title: Is HashMap always O(1)?
docs.text:
No.

Average complexity is O(1), but excessive hash collisions can degrade performance.

Before Java 8, worst-case complexity could become O(n).

Since Java 8, treeified buckets improve worst-case complexity to O(log n).

docs.title: Why is ConcurrentHashMap faster than Hashtable?
docs.text:
Hashtable uses a single lock for most operations.

ConcurrentHashMap allows multiple threads to access different regions of the map concurrently, significantly reducing lock contention.

docs.title: Can HashMap be used safely by multiple threads?
docs.text:
Not without external synchronization.

Concurrent modifications can cause:

  • Lost updates
  • Inconsistent reads
  • Internal structure corruption

For concurrent access, ConcurrentHashMap should be used.

docs.title: Key Points to Remember
docs.text:
• HashMap → Fast, non-thread-safe.
  • ConcurrentHashMap → Thread-safe, scalable, modern choice.
  • Hashtable → Thread-safe, legacy implementation, generally avoided.
  • HashMap uses buckets and hashing.
  • ConcurrentHashMap uses CAS and fine-grained synchronization.
  • Hashtable synchronizes the entire map.

docs.title: 2. What happens when executing String s = "hello";?
docs.text:
When the JVM encounters the string literal "hello", it first checks the String Pool.

  • If the string already exists, the existing instance is reused.
  • Otherwise, a new String object is created in the String Pool.

The variable s stores a reference to that pooled String.

docs.title: Why Strings Are Immutable
docs.text:
String immutability provides:

  • Thread safety
  • String Pooling
  • Hashcode caching
  • Security

Example:

String a = "hello";
String b = "hello";

Both variables reference the same String object.

docs.title: Heap
docs.text:
The heap stores:

  • Objects
  • Arrays

Characteristics:

  • Shared among all threads
  • Managed by the garbage collector
  • Configurable using -Xms and -Xmx

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
Each thread has its own stack.

The stack stores:

  • Method frames
  • Local variables
  • Object references

Characteristics:

  • One stack per thread
  • LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)

docs.title: Metaspace
docs.text:
Metaspace stores class metadata such as:

  • Class definitions
  • Methods
  • Fields
  • Annotations
  • Inheritance information

Characteristics:

  • Stored in native memory
  • Outside the heap
  • Replaced PermGen in Java 8

docs.title: 4. What is the difference between == and equals()?
==
docs.text:
Compares:

  • Primitive values
  • Object references

Example:

String a = new String("abc");
String b = new String("abc");

System.out.println(a == b);

Output:

false

docs.title: equals()
docs.text:
Compares logical equality.

Example:

System.out.println(a.equals(b));

Output:

true

docs.title: Important Rule
docs.text: Whenever equals() is overridden, hashCode() must also be overridden.

docs.title: 5. What is a memory leak in Java?
docs.text: A memory leak occurs when objects are no longer needed but remain reachable from GC roots.

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
• Static collections
  • Caches without eviction
  • ThreadLocal misuse
  • Unremoved listeners
  • Long-lived references

docs.title: Key Point
docs.text: Garbage Collection cannot remove objects that are still reachable.

docs.title: volatile
docs.text:
Provides:

  • Visibility guarantees
  • Ordering guarantees

Does not provide:

  • Atomicity

Example:

volatile int counter;
counter++;

The increment operation is still not thread-safe.

docs.title: synchronized
docs.text:
Provides:

  • Mutual exclusion
  • Visibility guarantees
  • Atomic execution of critical sections

Example:

synchronized(lock) {
    counter++;
}

docs.title: AtomicInteger
docs.text:
Provides lock-free thread-safe operations using CAS (Compare-And-Set).

Example:

AtomicInteger counter = new AtomicInteger();

counter.incrementAndGet();

docs.title: What is CAS?
docs.text:
CAS stands for Compare-And-Set.

Conceptually:

IF currentValue == expectedValue
    update value
ELSE
    fail

CAS is implemented using CPU-level atomic instructions such as:

CMPXCHG

on x86 processors.

docs.title: 7. What happens during a Full GC?
docs.text:
A Full GC performs garbage collection across the entire heap.

Typically includes:

  • Young Generation
  • Old Generation

Characteristics:

  • Stop-The-World pause
  • Application threads are paused
  • Memory is reclaimed

Frequent Full GCs may indicate:

  • Memory leaks
  • Insufficient heap size
  • Poor JVM tuning

docs.title: 8. What is a ClassLoader?
docs.text: A ClassLoader loads classes into JVM memory at runtime.

docs.title: Bootstrap ClassLoader
docs.text:
Loads core Java classes:

java.lang.String
java.util.List

docs.title: Platform ClassLoader
docs.text: Loads JDK platform libraries.

docs.title: Application ClassLoader
docs.text: Loads application classes from the classpath.

docs.title: Parent Delegation Model
docs.text:
Class loading follows this order:

Application
    ↓
Platform
    ↓
Bootstrap

Benefits:

  • Security
  • Prevents duplicate class loading

docs.title: 9. What happens when multiple threads modify the same HashMap?
docs.text:
HashMap is not thread-safe.

Possible issues:

  • Lost updates
  • Inconsistent reads
  • Corrupted internal state
  • Resize-related problems

docs.title: Concurrent Resize Problem
docs.text:
When a HashMap grows, it resizes and rehashes entries.

Concurrent modifications during resize can corrupt internal structures.

For concurrent access, use:

ConcurrentHashMap

docs.title: Optimistic Locking
docs.text:
Assumes conflicts are rare.

Typically implemented using:

@Version
private Long version;

Update process:

  1. Read current version
  2. Attempt update
  3. Verify version before commit

If the version changed:

OptimisticLockException

is thrown.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Better scalability
  • No database lock held

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text: • Updates may need retries

docs.title: Pessimistic Locking
docs.text:
Assumes conflicts are likely.

Database rows are locked immediately.

Example:

SELECT *
FROM account
FOR UPDATE;

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text: • Prevents concurrent updates

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text:
• Lower throughput
  • Potential deadlocks
  • Longer lock durations

docs.title: ExecutorService
docs.text:
Manages and executes tasks using a pool of reusable threads.

Example:

ExecutorService executor =
        Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);

executor.submit(() -> processPayment());

Benefits:

  • Thread reuse
  • Resource management
  • Better scalability

docs.title: ForkJoinPool
docs.text:
Designed for recursive divide-and-conquer workloads.

Process:

  1. Split large task (Fork)
  2. Execute subtasks in parallel
  3. Combine results (Join)

Example:

parallelStream()

often uses ForkJoinPool internally.

docs.title: Work Stealing
docs.text:
Each worker thread has its own task queue.

If a worker becomes idle:

Worker-1 queue -> empty
Worker-2 queue -> many tasks

Worker-1 can steal tasks from Worker-2.

Benefits:

  • Better CPU utilization
  • Improved load balancing

docs.title: CompletableFuture
docs.text:
Represents an asynchronous computation.

Example:

CompletableFuture<User> future =
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
        () -> userService.getUser()
    );

Supports chaining:

future.thenApply(...)
      .thenAccept(...)
      .exceptionally(...);

Allows asynchronous workflows without blocking.

docs.title: Relationship
docs.text:
• ExecutorService executes tasks.
  • ForkJoinPool is a specialized ExecutorService.
  • CompletableFuture represents asynchronous computations and typically uses ExecutorService or ForkJoinPool underneath.

docs.title: 12. What is a deadlock?
docs.text:
A deadlock occurs when two or more threads permanently wait for resources held by each other.

Example:

Thread A:

Holds Lock A
Waiting for Lock B

Thread B:

Holds Lock B
Waiting for Lock A

Neither thread can continue.

docs.title: Detection
docs.text:
Common tools:

jstack <pid>
  • VisualVM
  • Java Mission Control
  • Thread Dumps

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• Consistent lock ordering
  • Reduced nested locking
  • tryLock()
  • Lock acquisition timeouts

docs.title: 13. What is the difference between synchronized and ReentrantLock?
synchronized
docs.text:
Example:

synchronized(lock) {
    // critical section
}

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Simple
  • Automatic unlock
  • Less error-prone

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text: • Less flexible

docs.title: ReentrantLock
docs.text:
Example:

lock.lock();

try {
    // critical section
}
finally {
    lock.unlock();
}

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
Timed lock acquisition:

lock.tryLock();

Interruptible locking:

lock.lockInterruptibly();

Fairness:

new ReentrantLock(true);

Multiple Conditions:

Condition notEmpty;
Condition notFull;

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text:
• Must manually unlock
  • Easier to introduce bugs

docs.title: Condition Objects
docs.text:
Condition objects create separate wait queues.

Example:

  • Producers wait on notFull
  • Consumers wait on notEmpty

This avoids waking unrelated threads and improves efficiency.

docs.title: 14. How would you implement a thread-safe counter?
Using synchronized
docs.text:
public class Counter {

    private int counter;

    public synchronized int increment() {
        return ++counter;
    }
}

docs.title: Using AtomicInteger
docs.text:
public class Counter {

    private final AtomicInteger counter =
            new AtomicInteger();

    public int increment() {
        return counter.incrementAndGet();
    }
}

docs.title: High Throughput Systems
docs.text:
Preferred:

AtomicInteger

because it avoids lock contention.

For extremely high contention:

LongAdder

often provides better performance than AtomicInteger.

docs.title: Common Follow-Up Questions
docs.text:
• What is CAS?
  • How does ConcurrentHashMap work internally?
  • What is fine-grained locking?

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• JVM Architecture
  • Stack vs Heap
  • Metaspace
  • Stack Frames
  • Class Loading
  • Java Memory Model

docs.title: Interview Question
docs.text: Explain the JVM architecture and what happens when a Java application starts.

docs.title: Senior Answer
docs.text:
Java source code is compiled by the Java compiler (javac) into platform-independent bytecode (.class files). When the application starts, the JVM loads the required classes using the Class Loader subsystem.

The JVM allocates memory across several runtime data areas, including the Heap, Stack, Metaspace, Program Counter Register, and Native Method Stack.

The Execution Engine initially interprets the bytecode. Frequently executed ("hot") methods are then compiled into native machine code by the Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler for better performance.

Memory management is handled automatically by the Garbage Collector, which removes objects that are no longer reachable.

docs.title: Follow-up Questions
docs.text:
• What is bytecode?
  • Why is Java platform independent?
  • What is JIT?
  • What is a hot method?
  • What is the difference between Interpreter and JIT?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Saying the JVM executes Java source code.

✅ The JVM executes bytecode.

docs.title: Interview Question
docs.text: What is the difference between Stack and Heap memory?

docs.title: Senior Answer
docs.text:
The Stack is thread-local memory used to store method stack frames, local variables, primitive values, and object references. Memory is allocated and released automatically when methods are invoked and return.

The Heap is shared across all threads and stores objects and arrays. Objects remain in memory until they become unreachable, after which the Garbage Collector can reclaim them.

Stack allocation is faster because it follows a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) structure, while heap allocation requires memory management by the JVM.

docs.title: Follow-up Questions
docs.text:
• Where are objects stored?
  • Where are references stored?
  • Can the Heap cause StackOverflowError?
  • Can the Stack cause OutOfMemoryError?
  • Why is Stack faster?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Objects are stored on the Stack.

✅ Object references are on the Stack; objects themselves are on the Heap.

docs.title: Interview Question
docs.text: What is Metaspace?

docs.title: Senior Answer
docs.text:
Metaspace is the JVM memory area that stores class metadata, including class definitions, method metadata, field information, annotations, and runtime constant pools.

Starting with Java 8, Metaspace replaced the Permanent Generation (PermGen). Unlike PermGen, Metaspace uses native memory instead of the Java Heap, making it more flexible and reducing OutOfMemoryErrors caused by fixed-size metadata storage.

docs.title: Follow-up Questions
docs.text:
• What replaced PermGen?
  • Does Metaspace store objects?
  • Can Metaspace run out of memory?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Metaspace stores Java objects.

✅ It stores class metadata, not application objects.

docs.title: Interview Question
docs.text: What is a Stack Frame?

docs.title: Senior Answer
docs.text:
Every method invocation creates a new Stack Frame on the current thread's stack.

A Stack Frame contains:

  • Local variables
  • Operand stack
  • Return address
  • Method execution information

When the method completes, its Stack Frame is removed from the stack automatically.

Each thread has its own independent Stack, which is why local variables are thread-safe by default.

docs.title: Follow-up Questions
docs.text:
• What is stored inside a Stack Frame?
  • Why does recursion cause StackOverflowError?
  • Does each thread have its own Stack?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Stack Frames are shared between threads.

✅ Each thread owns its own Stack and Stack Frames.

docs.title: Interview Question
docs.text: Explain the Java Class Loading process.

docs.title: Senior Answer
docs.text:
Class loading consists of three main phases:

  1. Loading – The Class Loader locates the .class file and loads it into memory.
  2. Linking – The JVM verifies the bytecode, prepares static fields, and resolves symbolic references.
  3. Initialization – Static variables and static initialization blocks are executed.

Java uses a parent delegation model where class loaders delegate loading to their parent before attempting to load the class themselves. This prevents duplicate loading and improves security.

docs.title: Follow-up Questions
docs.text:
• What is the Bootstrap ClassLoader?
  • What is Parent Delegation?
  • Why is Parent Delegation important?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Classes are initialized immediately after compilation.

✅ Classes are initialized when first actively used.

docs.title: Interview Question
docs.text: What is the Java Memory Model?

docs.title: Senior Answer
docs.text:
The Java Memory Model defines how threads interact through memory and guarantees visibility, ordering, and atomicity of shared variables.

It specifies when changes made by one thread become visible to other threads and defines the happens-before relationship.

Synchronization mechanisms such as synchronized, volatile, locks, and atomic classes rely on the Java Memory Model to ensure thread safety.

docs.title: Follow-up Questions
docs.text:
• What is happens-before?
  • What does volatile guarantee?
  • Does volatile make operations atomic?
  • What visibility guarantees exist?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ volatile makes every operation thread-safe.

✅ volatile guarantees visibility and ordering, but not atomicity.

docs.title: Quick Revision
docs.text: Topic Key Takeaway JVM Executes bytecode using Interpreter and JIT Stack Thread-local, stores Stack Frames Heap Shared memory for objects Metaspace Stores class metadata Stack Frame Created for every method call Class Loading Loading → Linking → Initialization JMM Defines visibility, ordering and atomicity rules

docs.title: Homework
docs.text:
Be able to answer every question above without reading the notes.

Target answer length:

  • JVM Architecture → 2 minutes
  • Stack vs Heap → 2 minutes
  • Metaspace → 1 minute
  • Stack Frames → 1 minute
  • Class Loading → 2 minutes
  • Java Memory Model → 2 minutes

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
Heap stores objects and arrays and is shared among all threads.

Stack stores method frames, local variables, and references and is private to each thread.

Metaspace stores class metadata and resides in native memory outside the heap.

docs.title: Heap
docs.text:
Stores:

  • Objects
  • Arrays

Managed by the garbage collector.

Shared between all threads.

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
Stores:

  • Method calls
  • Local variables
  • References

Each thread has its own stack.

Follows LIFO behavior.

docs.title: Why are local variables thread-safe?
docs.text:
Each thread owns its own stack.

Local variables are not shared between threads.

docs.title: Metaspace
docs.text:
Stores:

  • Class definitions
  • Methods
  • Fields
  • Annotations
  • Bytecode metadata

docs.title: Is Metaspace part of the heap?
docs.text:
No.

Metaspace uses native memory outside the Java heap.

docs.title: What existed before Metaspace?
docs.text:
PermGen.

Java 8 replaced PermGen with Metaspace.

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
The String Pool is a special memory area used to store String literals.

If a String already exists in the pool, the JVM reuses it instead of creating a new object.

docs.title: Where is String Pool stored?
docs.text: In modern Java versions, the String Pool resides inside the heap.

docs.title: Why is String immutable?
docs.text:
• Thread safety
  • String pooling
  • Hashcode caching
  • Security

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
Full GC performs garbage collection across the entire heap.

Application threads are paused during the operation.

Frequent Full GC events usually indicate memory pressure or memory leaks.

docs.title: What is Stop-The-World?
docs.text: Application threads are temporarily paused while garbage collection is performed.

docs.title: Interview Answer
docs.text:
ClassLoaders load classes into memory at runtime.

Main ClassLoaders:

  • Bootstrap ClassLoader
  • Platform ClassLoader
  • Application ClassLoader

docs.title: Parent Delegation Model
docs.text:
A child class loader asks its parent to load a class first.

This prevents duplicate loading and improves security.

docs.title: What loads java.lang.String?
docs.text: Bootstrap ClassLoader.
